98 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

originated in an obscure Irish village. Mr. Doudney edited this, as
well as another older established periodical, the ““ Gospel Magazine,”
both of which circulated almost entirely in England, and the difficulty
of transit, and regular supply to their readers, was, as may well be
imagined very great, and one of the reasons given for the removal of
the press. In 1856 Mr. Doudney issued a ‘“ Pictorial Outline ™ of the
schools, containing wood-cuts of his printing house and composing
rooms, etc., and mentioning many of the works printed there. 1 sub-
join a list of the works prihted at Bonmahon, so far as I have been
able to ascertain them, as well as a summary of the numbers, taken
from the  Life of D. A. Doudney,” by his son (8vo., London, 1834),
some of which I have never seen or found any other record of. These
will give an idea of the character and quantity of the output. But in
addition to these there were many tracts and leaflets printed, which
have gone the way of all such ephemera, though some are reprinted in
the last work issued from Dr. (as he afterwards became) Doudney’s
pen ‘“ Ireland; Its Priests and its People ’” (8vo, London, 1892.) No
doubt the management of such a concern must have encroached upon
the t,imé necessary for his parochial work, and in 1858 he resigned.
As he says himself in the last-mentioned work: “ Upon the comple-
tion of the printing and issue of Dr. Gill’s commentary, numerous
other works were put in hand, but the sale of such works did not
meet the expenditure. Hence as my Diocesan would not sanction ‘mY
spending three months a year in England, in order to secure & wider

practical interest in my work, I was compelled to resign my curacy

in order to preven$ my being pecuniarily involved for want of sale of
the various works which had passed through the press.” Accordiﬂ'gl)':
in 1858, he ceased operations and sold his plant to Messrs. Gollm'g-
ridge of London, and departed after a residence of eleven years m

~ Ireland. It may not be without interest to add two facts. He was
~afterwards appointed to a living in Bedminster, where he also foun

ded
‘a printing school, from which upwards of three hundred thousaﬂd
works (large and small)’ were igsued, and that his brother Greorges

originally a tailor, was also ordained into the Irish Church, having

_whilst a student at the University, taught himself the Irish lang“i‘lf: ;

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 101

The information contained in this brochure is most valuable and
we believe equally reliable. Its data concerning Irish literature, peri-
odicals and literary societies is perhaps unique, and was utilised to the
full by John Power in his works. For instance, he refers to a ‘“ School
Master’s Magazine,”” published in Armagh about 1840. Who can tell
of it? He gives a short account of the Ulster Gaelic Society in 1830, of
which Lord Downshire was president. This was Ferguson’s stimulant
He deplores the neglect of Irish literature quoting Byron’s verse:—

For happy are they now reposing afar

Thy Grattan, thy Curramn, thy Sheridan, all

Who for years were the chiefs in the eloquent war,

And redeemed, if they have not retarded thy fall—

Yes, happy are they in their cold English graves. - ,

He notes the ‘“ Belfast Magazine *’ of 1808-14, initiated by Dr. Dren-
nan and John Templeton. John Lawless’ *° Ulster Register  oi 1816
is mentioned, ag is the ‘° Belfast Magazine and Literary Journal >’ of
Professor Cairn’s (1825), and Charles H. Teeling’s short lived ‘“ Ulster

Magazine’’ of 1830, and the ‘‘ New Belfast Magazine,”” run by the -

Institution youths in 1833-4.
We only accentuate the Northern productlons, but other centres

are equally noted by the writer.
-~ FRANCIS JOSEPH BIGGER, M.R.I.A.

Mr. Bigger’s note on this writer is full of interest, as everything he

writes always is, and it gives me, for one, a curious bit of information -

as to the authorship of an anonymous brochure that I have often

seen. I daresay that Mr. Bigger is aware that ' Montrrmnery' wrote S =

other books, including ““ A Short Life of Thomas Moore.” I regret to

GOSSIP.

The death of Dr. John Dowden, Bishop of Edinburgh, is announced.
He was the eldest brother of Professor Dowden, T.C.D., and born in
Cork in 1840. He was educated at Queen’s College, Cork and Trinity,
where he had a brilliant academical career. Originally ordained intothe
Irish Church, he was transferred to Scotland and elected to the
Bishopric in 1866. He became an enthusiastic antiquarian and pub-
lished many works relative to the history and literature of his adopted
country.

News reaches me from Belfast that the ﬂrrangements for the cele-
bration of the Ferguson Centenary are now completed. There will be
an exhibition of MSS. sketches, books and portraits of him and his
circle. It is intended to produce two of his plays, and as a lasting
memorial to place a medallion or inscription on the front of the house
in High Street where he was born. In T.C.D., the Rev. Richard Seaver
will deliver a commemoration address on his life and work on Trinity

the occasion with a “ Ferguson Night,” on 5th March, and lastly, I

\ issue a ““ Ferguson Centenary Number.”

five vears. Ireland has a right to be proud of this work, and ‘its
onlie and sole begetter,” for though Dr. Wright had the misfortune to.
be born in Yorkshire, his father, the late Dr. C. H. H. Wright, was one
of the greatest scholars in the Irish church, and he himself received his
education at the old * Inst.” in Belfast and T.C.D. His love of books.
atiracted him to the service of the National Library, whence he was

What a fascination Swift seems to possess for the biographer. To
the long list already extant, another memoir of him as a man, and a
studv of him as an author, is announced by Messrs. Methuen. Miss
Sophie S. Smith is the writer of the book which closes with a chapter
on his work in Ireland.

Some fresh material of value relating to the Irish rebellion of
1798 1s promised in “ The War in Wexford,” by Mr. H. F. B. Wheeler
and Mr. A. M. Broadley, announced by Mr. John Lane. The book is
based on hitherto unpublished documents, including the correspond-
ence of the first Earl of Mount Norrig, the Detail Book of the Camolin
Cavalry, and the Diary of Mrs. Brownrigg, a lady who was for a time
Imprisoned on the ship commanded by Captain Dixon, saw the mass-
acre of Wexford Bridge, and was detained in the town until its relief
by Moore’s troops. Irom these, and from other contemporary writers,
the authors have drawn up a narrative which aims at holding the
Scales evenly between loyalists and rebels. The book will appear—ap-
Propriately—on St. Patrick’s Day.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

An Original Night was held on February 5th, when Rutherford
Mayne’s play “The Troth,” was presented with great success, the
different parts being played by Joseph Campbell, Alice O’Dea, Whit-
ford Kane, and A. E. Morrovw.

The acting was very realistic, the County Down atmosphere being
admirably rendered. The play, a fine spirited, piece of work, was
greatly appreciated by the audience, which was one of the largest seen
recently. In addition, an excellent musical programme was gone
through, and Miss Wheeler recited some poems of her own, suggested
by a visit to the Aran Islands.

On the 17th February there was a Literary Tea-Table, at which
Miss Eleanor Hull introduced the subject of ““Irish Folk-Lore Col-
lecting,”” and gave an interesting account of the folk-lore of different
parts of Ireland, and of its connection with that of other Celtic coun-
tries. Miss Burn (President of the English Folk-Lore Society), Mr.
Graves, Mr. Fahy, Mr. Buckley, Mrs. Thompson, and Mr. Moses made
irjteresting contributions to the discussion which followed. Miss
Hull announced the formation of a Society in Dublin, for the purpose
of collecting and arranging Irish folk-lore.

~ On the 19th, Mr. Graves lectured on “ Music in Irish Life.”” Mr.
Stephen Gwynn, M.P., was in the chair, and made an amusing speech
as to the connection between music and politics in Ireland. Mr.
Graves traced the history of Irish music from the earliestl times, and
showed the immense influence that it had exercised on the music of
other countries, though he declared that the air of the ‘“ Wearing of

| ‘the Green,” was originally Scottish. He dealt with the collecting

work done by Petrie and Bunting, and more recently by Dr. Joyce-

~ Mr. Graves had got together a number of talented artistes, and vocal

and mstrumental items, were rendered, illustrating Trish music in the

~ heroic and medizeval periods in the 18th and 19th Centuries, and ab

“"-M&Y Coleman, Mrs. Harry Bedford, Miss Cecilia Kemp, Madam®
